name: Immersive sound: from THX to surround  sound
description: The THX sound became a symbol of immersive cinema, signalling pristine audio quality. From Return of the Jedi to videogames, creators explored spatial sound, dynamic mixing, and atmospheric audio, showing how sound shapes experience, emotion, and perception across multiple media forms.

key_work.title: Immersive sound in cinema: THX
key_work.brief_description:
When Star Wars hit cinemas in the late 1970s and 80s, sound engineer Tomlinson Holman noticed a problem: the rich, detailed audio created during production wasn’t being heard properly in theatres. His solution was THX – a new sound standard that ensured films played exactly as filmmakers intended.
When Return of the Jedi (1983) premiered, only THX-certified cinemas could show it. Audiences were greeted by the now-iconic ‘Deep Note’ – a rising wave of tones swelling into a mighty D major chord. It shook the room and set the stage for the galaxy far, far away – as well as a new level of excellence in cinema sound.
Fun fact: THX was named after George Lucas’s first film, THX 1138 (1971).

constellations_primary.name: Immersive sound: from THX to surround  sound
constellations_primary.description: The THX sound became a symbol of immersive cinema, signalling pristine audio quality. From Return of the Jedi to videogames, creators explored spatial sound, dynamic mixing, and atmospheric audio, showing how sound shapes experience, emotion, and perception across multiple media forms.

end.title: Return of the Jedi
end.creator_credit: Richard Marquand, Howard Kazanjian and Lucasfilm, Ltd.
end.brief_description: The conclusion of the “Star Wars” trilogy begins with the rescue of Hans Solo from the evil clutches of Jabba the Hutt. The Galactic Empire has resurrected the Death Star and plans to eliminate the Rebel Alliance once and for all. Luke Skywalker, grappling with the secret that Darth Vader is his father, who was turned evil by the Dark Side of the Force, must confront the Emperor in one final battle. From the seedy den of Jabba the Hutt, to the luscious green peacefulness of the planet Endor with its Ewoks, “The Return of the Jedi” is an entralling action-packed special effects adventure.

constellations_other.name: Scene-stealing giants
constellations_other.description: We Live in an Ocean of Air takes viewers inside the cycle of life through a giant sequoia (commonly known as a giant redwood). Find out how their ancient awesomeness has seen them loom over screen culture.

constellations_other.name: Immersive sound: from THX to surround  sound
constellations_other.description: The THX sound became a symbol of immersive cinema, signalling pristine audio quality. From Return of the Jedi to videogames, creators explored spatial sound, dynamic mixing, and atmospheric audio, showing how sound shapes experience, emotion, and perception across multiple media forms.

end.creator_credit_for_label: Richard Marquand, Howard Kazanjian and Lucasfilm, Ltd.

links.description: Lucasfilm restricted Return of the Jedi to THX-certified cinemas, ensuring pristine sound. Its orchestral score, sound effects, and mixing demonstrated how immersive audio enhances narrative engagement, showing audiences that spatial and dynamic sound could transform their cinematic experience.

end.title: Jurassic Park
end.creator_credit: Gerald R. Molen and Kathleen Kennedy
end.brief_description: An entrepreneur creates a theme park featuring living dinosaurs drawn from prehistoric DNA. Before opening day, he invites experts and his two grandchildren to experience the park and help calm anxious investors – a tough task when the dinosaurs escape.

constellations_other.name: Representing disasters
constellations_other.description: The devastating Indian Ocean tsunami was captured by many people on personal devices and home cameras, with the footage broadcast to the world. The event also inspired feature films that are part of a long tradition of disaster films, which often push the boundaries of VFX.

constellations_other.name: Immersive sound: from THX to surround  sound
constellations_other.description: The THX sound became a symbol of immersive cinema, signalling pristine audio quality. From Return of the Jedi to videogames, creators explored spatial sound, dynamic mixing, and atmospheric audio, showing how sound shapes experience, emotion, and perception across multiple media forms.

links.description: Jurassic Park used surround sound to make dinosaurs feel physically present. Like Return of the Jedi, its layered audio design placed viewers inside the environment, demonstrating that spatial sound can heighten tension, realism, and emotional investment in cinematic worlds.

start.title: Jurassic Park
start.creator_credit: Gerald R. Molen and Kathleen Kennedy
start.brief_description: An entrepreneur creates a theme park featuring living dinosaurs drawn from prehistoric DNA. Before opening day, he invites experts and his two grandchildren to experience the park and help calm anxious investors – a tough task when the dinosaurs escape.

end.title: DOOM
end.credit_line: Courtesy id Software and ZeniMax Media Inc
end.brief_description:
Doom (1993) is widely considered one of the most influential videogames of all time and is credited with inspiring the ‘first-person shooter’ (FPS) genre.

In 1996 the game’s developers, id Software, released the source code for Doom under a non-commercial license, to encourage porting, modding, and the creation of new games.

Before game engines were commercially available, most games were made with bespoke engines – the software framework used to create a game – or created as mods of existing engines.

The release of the source code not only extended the life of the game itself, but it also gave rise to an entirely new medium – the creative challenge of getting Doom running on anything. The blog It Runs Doom catalogues the unlikely devices that can run the game, from vintage videogame consoles to a digital camera to a tractor interface.

It Runs Doom

In 1997 id Software released the source code for their landmark game Doom (1993), making it free for use by anyone. The elegant simplicity and low hardware requirements of the code means that it runs on an extremely wide range of technology.

In this display you can see the work of Wētā Workshop graphics engineer James Brown, who got Doom running on a tiny 3D-printed toy block. Brown’s device is powered not by a battery, but by conductive bricks which were first designed for LEGO’s monorail set.

constellations_other.name: Immersive sound: from THX to surround  sound
constellations_other.description: The THX sound became a symbol of immersive cinema, signalling pristine audio quality. From Return of the Jedi to videogames, creators explored spatial sound, dynamic mixing, and atmospheric audio, showing how sound shapes experience, emotion, and perception across multiple media forms.

links.description: Doom pioneered stereo and digital audio in games, using positional sound to immerse players. Like Jurassic Park, its sound design creates spatial awareness and tension, showing that immersive audio is essential not only in film but in interactive, gameplay-driven environments.

start.credit_line: Courtesy id Software and ZeniMax Media Inc

end.title: Gravity
end.creator_credit: Alfonso Cuarón, Jany Temime, Mark Sanger, David Heyman, Esperanto Filmoj, Heyday Films, Warner Bros. Pictures, Andy Nicholson, Adam Paschke, Adam Potter, Alex Meddick, Ben Paschke, Damien Thaller, Daniel Thompson, Dan Wills, Dennis Jones, Ian Cope, Matthew Shaw, Nick Pill, Paul Kirwan, Premamurti Paetsch, Sam Hancock, Sarah McLauchlan, Tim Bowman, Tony Clark and Jonás Cuarón
end.headline_credit: United Kingdom and United States of America, 2013
end.brief_description: Dr. Ryan Stone, a brilliant medical engineer, is on her first Shuttle mission, with veteran astronaut Matt Kowalsky in command of his last flight before retiring. But on a seemingly routine spacewalk, disaster strikes.

end.headline_credit_for_label: United Kingdom and United States of America, 2013

links.description: Alfonso Cuarón’s Gravity relies on diegetic sound and silence to situate viewers in space. Like Doom, its precise use of audio cues shapes perception and immersion

end.title: Hellblade: Senua's Sacrifice
end.brief_description: Set in the Viking age, a broken Celtic warrior embarks on a haunting vision quest into Viking Hell to fight for the soul of her dead lover.

constellations_other.name: Immersive sound: from THX to surround  sound
constellations_other.description: The THX sound became a symbol of immersive cinema, signalling pristine audio quality. From Return of the Jedi to videogames, creators explored spatial sound, dynamic mixing, and atmospheric audio, showing how sound shapes experience, emotion, and perception across multiple media forms.

links.description: Hellblade employs binaural audio to immerse players in Senua’s mind, turning internal voices into spatialised, 3D experiences. Like Gravity, the meticulous placement of sound shapes emotional response, illustrating immersive audio as a storytelling tool beyond traditional visual media.

start.title: Hellblade: Senua's Sacrifice
start.brief_description: Set in the Viking age, a broken Celtic warrior embarks on a haunting vision quest into Viking Hell to fight for the soul of her dead lover.

From Return of the Jedi to videogames, creators explored spatial sound, dynamic mixing, and atmospheric audio, showing how sound shapes experience, emotion, and perception across multiple media forms.

end.title: Fantasia
end.creator_credit: Bill Roberts, Paul Satterfield and Walt Disney
end.brief_description: Walt Disney’s timeless masterpiece is an extravaganza of sight and sound! See the music come to life, hear the pictures burst into song and experience the excitement that is Fantasia over and over again.

constellations_other.name: Flower on the wind
constellations_other.description: Explore the use of flowers in the moving image as an aesthetic and metaphor.

constellations_other.name: Immersive sound: from THX to surround  sound
constellations_other.description: The THX sound became a symbol of immersive cinema, signalling pristine audio quality. From Return of the Jedi to videogames, creators explored spatial sound, dynamic mixing, and atmospheric audio, showing how sound shapes experience, emotion, and perception across multiple media forms.

end.creator_credit_for_label: Bill Roberts, Paul Satterfield and Walt Disney

links.description: Disney’s Fantasia uses music as both narrative and spatial guide, anticipating immersive audio ideals like those later used in Hellblade.
